body.value: Questions about if racism exists in Peru

"What should we, Black people, do if we do not even know how to write? To clean the bathroom in the big hotel and sweep?" Nicomedes Santa Cruz's poem about the social condition of oppression of Blackness

"Racism is maybe the worst heritage that we have from the colonial society. It is a way of considering people by the color of their skin, more than what we have inside. It is more about the external appearance than what we have as humans. And it is true that racism has changed in a certain sense. If before, racism justify that the men of African origin was enslaved, and we used to say yes because it did not have certain features. As a consequence, it was supposed to be right in that situation. Later, with the formation of our Republic, racism was not the same to justify that the African men were slaves, but second-class citizens. For instance, this person cannot join the Navy. not because he is Black, mind you, but because, as you know, one must be "respectable"; one must have the right family background and a solid income. And naturally, he doesn't fit the bill. Instead, the attitude is: "Well, you're Black. You're fine for sports, or maybe for dancing." Thus, racism has shifted somewhat in its tone and manner of expression, but it remains fundamentally unchanged in its essence. Consequently, we are still effectively dragging this legacy behind us, because this mentality persisted, because the old hacienda system endured, and because we continue to inhabit the poorest areas, leaving our social status essentially unchanged. In reality, to speak of the poor in Peru is, by and large, to speak of Black people; there is a very strong correlation between poverty and race in our country."  Jose "Pepe" Luciano was an activist and Afro-Peruvian researcher about the social conditions of Black people in Peru. In this phrase, Pepe brings attention to the changes in racism from the colonial period to the early 90s.

"I went to Lima to look for work, you see. So, another employee, since there were several of them, came to attend to me. Then another employee walked in and said, "Alright," then, stay put; let the lady of the house speak with you. The moment the lady saw me, the only thing she said was, "What have you come here for?" I replied, "Ma'am, you know... I've come to work. I saw your advertisement, and I'd like to apply". But the lady said no. She told me, "No." I asked her, "Ma'am, why? You need an employee!" She replied, "Well, you know how it is. " She tried to change the subject, perhaps so as not to hurt my feelings or cause me offense. But in the end, she came right out and told me that "dark skin simply didn't fit into the environment they lived in." In this testimony, by a Black woman, we can not only understand the experience of racism and the lack of opportunities that Black women faced, but also find in domestic labor a way of finding development opportunities.

"Indeed, racism does exist in Peru, and it exists because our avenues for advancement are limited. To gain access to any given sphere, we are regularly compelled to prove that we are superior to everyone else. That is to say, while everyone must exert effort to access something or achieve a specific objective, as doing so always requires work, the effort *we* must expend, at any level, invariably exceeds that of the average person. In short, opportunities are scarce precisely *because* of racism. For instance, if a white person seeks employment at a company, they are accepted; a Black person, however, might not be. This is a frequent occurrence. Let's take, for example, a job application submitted to a government agency: people of color face discrimination. Ultimately, I believe that equal opportunities do not exist for people of color and white people; for the former, every opportunity and everything they have had to accomplish has required far more effort than it has for anyone else." Susana Matute Charun, a politician and activist, reflects on the implications that racism has in limiting the opportunities of Black people.
"Everything I had to, I had to work harder with more effort." In this testimony, Susana reflects on the extra effort she had to put in to accomplish things as a Black woman in Peru.

"The problem was only in place. I thought for a long time, well, I didn’t realize that it was my skin". In this testimony, Susana reflects that she did not understand the weight of the color of her kin in her social interactions completely. It is not about that the color of her skin being something bad, but the social constrcution that Blackness has based on the color of the skin.

The stereotypical representations of Blackness and whiteness in public media. Afro-Peruvians are represented through stereotypical images such as the "Mammy figure", while whiteness is portrayed as successful, beautiful, and clean.
